{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_01Adnx3WH2B2PCBb6MUMF5Z9","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01MFJXyHLrJVFWZNkqx9qeeB","name":"Read","input":{"file_path":"/app/papers/PMC4890827.md"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":274,"cache_read_input_tokens":28908,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":274,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"42ad976e-0d29-447c-8cde-f6e624ff3619","uuid":"6d0842fe-9882-4610-b8eb-3c85e94b8e81"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_018JRA24u1s4FctbkGmJtrXU","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Safe treatment of thiopurine S-methyltransferase deficient Crohn’s disease patients with azathioprine\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** B A Kaskas, E Louis, U Hindorf, E Schaeffeler, J Deflandre, F Graepler, K Schmiegelow, M Gregor, U M Zanger, M Eichelbaum, M Schwab\n     5→**Journal:** Gut\n     6→**Date:** 2003 Jan\n     7→**DOI:** [10.1136/gut.52.1.140](https://doi.org/10.1136/gut.52.1.140)\n     8→**PMID:** 12477776\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC1773505\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1773505/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1773505/pdf/gut05200140.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1773505/pdf/gut05200140.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→Thiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) deficient patients develop life threatening haematotoxicity (for example, pancytopenia) when treated with a standard dose of azathioprine (AZA) and 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) due to excessive accumulation of cytotoxic metabolites. At present, it is generally recommended that these patients should not receive AZA or 6-MP treatment for inflammatory bowel disease. We report for the first time that Crohn’s disease patients with TPMT deficiency can be successfully treated with AZA. We illustrate this with three cases where treatment has been successful and toxicity has been avoided by carefully titrating the drug dose. Thus very low TPMT activity demands pharmacogenetically guided dosing.\n    16→\n    17→Keywords: inflammatory bowel diseases, thiopurine S-methyltransferase, azathioprine\n    18→\n    19→## CASE REPORTS\n    20→\n    21→### Case No 1\n    22→\n    23→A 32 year old man diagnosed with Crohn’s disease aged 26 (Vienna classification proposal[7](#r7) A1) was shown to have terminal ileitis and colitis (L3) as well as enteric fistulae (B3) in May 1992. He was started on 1.3 mg/kg body weight AZA daily. Eight weeks later, he was admitted for severe myelosuppression (haemoglobin 3.8 g/dl; leucocytes 3400 /mm[3](#r3) (nadir 1270/mm[3](#r3)); thrombocytes 17 000/mm[3](#r3)) and AZA therapy was stopped. Full blood count normalised after three weeks and the patient was discharged. Subsequently, his disease was poorly controlled with various doses of oral steroids. In January 1996, he had a descending colostomy and resection of enteric fistulae. Three months later he was restarted on 0.29 mg/kg body weight AZA daily when his abdominal symptoms recurred and a new arthropathy developed despite steroids. Two months later, on endoscopy, he was free of inflammation and a re-anastomosis of the colostomy was successfully performed. He remained on 0.29 mg/kg body weight AZA for a further five months. Repeat full blood counts were normal. TPMT activity in red blood cells (RBC) was measured, as described previously,[8](#r8) and was found to be very low (2 nmol 6-methylthioguanine [6-MTG]/g Hb/h) which reflects the patient’s homozygous mutant TPMT genotype (**3A/*3A*).[9](#r9)\n    24→\n    25→### Case No 2\n    26→\n    27→A 21 year old woman with a six year (A1) history of histologically proven Crohn’s disease (inflammation (B1) in the terminal ileum and caecum (L3)) was started on 1 mg/kg body weight AZA in September 1997 after two flare ups while tapering methylpredisolone. After four weeks, when the AZA dose was increased to 1.5 mg/kg body weight, mild leukopenia (2510/mm[3](#r3)) was noticed and the dose was reduced to 1 mg/kg body weight. During the following year the patient remained in remission. In March 1999, when she developed tonsillitis with moderate leukopenia (3130/mm[3](#r3)), AZA was stopped and the patient recovered. In August 1999, methylprednisolone was restarted together with AZA. In view of the repeated episodes of haematological side effects while on standard dosages, a much lower dose of 12.5 mg (0.25 mg/kg body weight) AZA was given daily. When measured three weeks later, the concentration of 6-TGN in RBC was unexpectedly high (1014 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). AZA was reduced to 10 mg/day and repeat measurements demonstrated 6-TGN had decreased (679 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). Methylprednisolone was subsequently replaced by 9 mg budesonide daily. A further four months later, in May 2000, the patient received a single infusion of infliximab (5 mg/kg body weight) because of a flare up. Since then she has been in continuous remission for more than 12 months on just 8 mg (0.16 mg/kg body weight) AZA and 9 mg budesonide daily without adverse effects. TPMT activity was found to be very low (< 2 nmol 6-MTG/g Hb/h) which is in keeping with her homozygous mutant TPMT genotype (**3A/*3A*).\n    28→\n    29→### Case No 3\n    30→\n    31→In October 1993, a 22 year old man (B2, L3), who had been diagnosed with Crohn’s disease 10 years previously, was started on 0.71 mg/kg body weight AZA. Subsequently, steroids were gradually tapered off and, in September 1994, completely withdrawn. The patient remained asymptomatic on 0.71 mg/kg body weight AZA daily. Leucocyte count was in the range 3600 to 5400/mm[3](#r3) except for two short episodes (2800 and 2900/mm[3](#r3)). Phenotyping revealed TPMT deficiency in April 2000. Thereafter, 6-TGN levels were measured repeatedly (748–1144 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC) with methylated metabolites being undetectable. After AZA dose adjustment to just 0.26 mg/kg body weight daily, 6-TGN levels were somewhat lower (797 and 884 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). The patient remained well. TPMT activity was confirmed to be very low (<2 nmol 6-MTG/g Hb/h). Genotyping revealed compound heterozygous TPMT alleles (**3A/*3C*).\n    32→\n    33→## DISCUSSION\n    34→\n    35→TPMT is a pharmacogenetic polymorphism which should be routinely considered to safely and effectively administer thiopurines in whatever medical specialty they are employed. Prospective pheno-/genotyping of TPMT has become standard practice in major cancer treatment centres such as the Mayo Clinic (Rochester, Minnesota, USA) and St Jude’s Children’s Research Hospital (Memphis, Tennessee, USA).[11](#r11) The concept of pharmacogenetic based medicine has been proposed recently.[10,](#r10)[12](#r12)\n    36→\n    37→The cases presented demonstrate that TPMT deficiency in patients with IBD per se does not preclude thiopurine therapy and hence offers a further therapeutic option for this group of patients. It illustrates what could be gained from prospective screening for *TPMT* polymorphism. Furthermore, the cases presented prove that methylated metabolites are not essential for the therapeutic action of thiopurines in Crohn’s disease.\n    38→\n    39→In patients who finally respond to thioguanines, it has been shown that an increase in 6-TGN levels correlates with clinical response and consequently a target concentration of 6-TGN level could be used as a therapeutic guide for patients on AZA or 6-MP.[13,](#r13)[14](#r14) As a large number of patients already respond to 2 mg/kg AZA (or 1 mg/kg 6-MP) despite 6-TGN levels lower than the proposed threshold, this may be mainly applicable to patients not responding to standard doses.[15](#r15)\n    40→\n    41→On the assumption that 6-TGNs are the main active compounds in IBD, TPMT deficient patients should, if anything, be easier to treat with AZA as they lack a competing metabolic pathway. In addition, these patients may develop fewer gastrointestinal side effects such as hepatotoxicity because high TPMT activity can lead to accumulation of 6-methylmercaptopurine with a consequent increased risk of developing hepatotoxicity.[13](#r13) If, on the contrary, methylated metabolites were essential for the therapeutic action of thiopurines in IBD disease, no therapeutic effect should have been observed in our TPMT deficient patients.\n    42→\n    43→The precise mechanisms of thiopurine action in lymphocytes are still a matter of debate. Incorporation of 6-TGN into DNA may trigger cell apoptosis and seems to be essential for cytotoxicity in leukaemia derived cell lines. However, it has been shown that TPMT methylates 6-thioinosine-5′-monophosphate (TIMP), yielding the S-methylated derivative (methylTIMP) which is at least three times more potent an inhibitor of de novo purine synthesis than 6-TGN. This represents an alternative mechanism for cytotoxicity.[16](#r16) In order to achieve a particular therapeutically active percentage of 6-TGN incorporation into DNA, higher 6-TGN levels should be necessary in the case of TPMT deficiency which itself causes absence of methylTIMP. Despite a lower AZA dosage, it is therefore not surprising that minimal effective 6-TGN levels in TPMT deficient patients are higher than in TPMT wild-type patients.\n    44→\n    45→Furthermore, although investigations with human leukaemic cells overexpressing TPMT have recently shown that methylmercaptopurine nucleotides decrease leukaemia cell viability independent of thiopurine incorporation into cellular DNA,[16](#r16) our report demonstrates that this mechanism is not essential for therapeutic efficacy in TPMT deficient patients with Crohn’s disease and AZA therapy.\n    46→\n    47→Reports of a ninefold increased risk of secondary malignancy in TPMT heterozygous children treated with thiopurines for remission maintenance in ALL[17](#r17) is a major concern. This risk was shown to be associated with very high 6-TGN levels which contribute to much increased genomic thioguanine incorporation. This risk is most probably conditional on the simultaneous exposure to other cytotoxic drugs (for example, topoisomerase-2 inhibitors or alkylating agents) and may therefore not apply to IBD patients. None the less, as a precaution, repeated determinations of 6-TGN levels and subsequent titration to the lowest effective dose should be routinely performed when TPMT deficient patients are treated with thiopurines.\n    48→\n    49→Our observations should also be applicable to the recently described use of 6-thioguanine in IBD.[18,](#r18)[19](#r19)\n    50→\n    51→We propose to investigate whether the introduction of a clinically effective and specific TPMT inhibitor could increase the overall response rate to thiopurine treatment while reducing toxicity.\n    52→\n    53→It is to be hoped that nature’s knocking out of a seemingly functionless enzyme may lead us towards a more individually tailored optimisation of drug treatment.\n    54→\n    55→## Acknowledgments\n    56→\n    57→We are indebted to Dr J Steel (Plymouth, UK) for reading the manuscript. Dr E Louis is a research associate at the FNRS Belgium. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"42ad976e-0d29-447c-8cde-f6e624ff3619","uuid":"07e7e770-71ed-483d-a54b-c8a33a6250c6","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/app/papers/PMC1773505.md","content":"# Safe treatment of thiopurine S-methyltransferase deficient Crohn’s disease patients with azathioprine\n\n## Metadata\n**Authors:** B A Kaskas, E Louis, U Hindorf, E Schaeffeler, J Deflandre, F Graepler, K Schmiegelow, M Gregor, U M Zanger, M Eichelbaum, M Schwab\n**Journal:** Gut\n**Date:** 2003 Jan\n**DOI:** [10.1136/gut.52.1.140](https://doi.org/10.1136/gut.52.1.140)\n**PMID:** 12477776\n**PMCID:** PMC1773505\n**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1773505/\n**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1773505/pdf/gut05200140.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1773505/pdf/gut05200140.pdf)\n\n## Abstract\n\nThiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) deficient patients develop life threatening haematotoxicity (for example, pancytopenia) when treated with a standard dose of azathioprine (AZA) and 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) due to excessive accumulation of cytotoxic metabolites. At present, it is generally recommended that these patients should not receive AZA or 6-MP treatment for inflammatory bowel disease. We report for the first time that Crohn’s disease patients with TPMT deficiency can be successfully treated with AZA. We illustrate this with three cases where treatment has been successful and toxicity has been avoided by carefully titrating the drug dose. Thus very low TPMT activity demands pharmacogenetically guided dosing.\n\nKeywords: inflammatory bowel diseases, thiopurine S-methyltransferase, azathioprine\n\n## CASE REPORTS\n\n### Case No 1\n\nA 32 year old man diagnosed with Crohn’s disease aged 26 (Vienna classification proposal[7](#r7) A1) was shown to have terminal ileitis and colitis (L3) as well as enteric fistulae (B3) in May 1992. He was started on 1.3 mg/kg body weight AZA daily. Eight weeks later, he was admitted for severe myelosuppression (haemoglobin 3.8 g/dl; leucocytes 3400 /mm[3](#r3) (nadir 1270/mm[3](#r3)); thrombocytes 17 000/mm[3](#r3)) and AZA therapy was stopped. Full blood count normalised after three weeks and the patient was discharged. Subsequently, his disease was poorly controlled with various doses of oral steroids. In January 1996, he had a descending colostomy and resection of enteric fistulae. Three months later he was restarted on 0.29 mg/kg body weight AZA daily when his abdominal symptoms recurred and a new arthropathy developed despite steroids. Two months later, on endoscopy, he was free of inflammation and a re-anastomosis of the colostomy was successfully performed. He remained on 0.29 mg/kg body weight AZA for a further five months. Repeat full blood counts were normal. TPMT activity in red blood cells (RBC) was measured, as described previously,[8](#r8) and was found to be very low (2 nmol 6-methylthioguanine [6-MTG]/g Hb/h) which reflects the patient’s homozygous mutant TPMT genotype (**3A/*3A*).[9](#r9)\n\n### Case No 2\n\nA 21 year old woman with a six year (A1) history of histologically proven Crohn’s disease (inflammation (B1) in the terminal ileum and caecum (L3)) was started on 1 mg/kg body weight AZA in September 1997 after two flare ups while tapering methylpredisolone. After four weeks, when the AZA dose was increased to 1.5 mg/kg body weight, mild leukopenia (2510/mm[3](#r3)) was noticed and the dose was reduced to 1 mg/kg body weight. During the following year the patient remained in remission. In March 1999, when she developed tonsillitis with moderate leukopenia (3130/mm[3](#r3)), AZA was stopped and the patient recovered. In August 1999, methylprednisolone was restarted together with AZA. In view of the repeated episodes of haematological side effects while on standard dosages, a much lower dose of 12.5 mg (0.25 mg/kg body weight) AZA was given daily. When measured three weeks later, the concentration of 6-TGN in RBC was unexpectedly high (1014 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). AZA was reduced to 10 mg/day and repeat measurements demonstrated 6-TGN had decreased (679 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). Methylprednisolone was subsequently replaced by 9 mg budesonide daily. A further four months later, in May 2000, the patient received a single infusion of infliximab (5 mg/kg body weight) because of a flare up. Since then she has been in continuous remission for more than 12 months on just 8 mg (0.16 mg/kg body weight) AZA and 9 mg budesonide daily without adverse effects. TPMT activity was found to be very low (< 2 nmol 6-MTG/g Hb/h) which is in keeping with her homozygous mutant TPMT genotype (**3A/*3A*).\n\n### Case No 3\n\nIn October 1993, a 22 year old man (B2, L3), who had been diagnosed with Crohn’s disease 10 years previously, was started on 0.71 mg/kg body weight AZA. Subsequently, steroids were gradually tapered off and, in September 1994, completely withdrawn. The patient remained asymptomatic on 0.71 mg/kg body weight AZA daily. Leucocyte count was in the range 3600 to 5400/mm[3](#r3) except for two short episodes (2800 and 2900/mm[3](#r3)). Phenotyping revealed TPMT deficiency in April 2000. Thereafter, 6-TGN levels were measured repeatedly (748–1144 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC) with methylated metabolites being undetectable. After AZA dose adjustment to just 0.26 mg/kg body weight daily, 6-TGN levels were somewhat lower (797 and 884 pmol/8×10^8^ RBC). The patient remained well. TPMT activity was confirmed to be very low (<2 nmol 6-MTG/g Hb/h). Genotyping revealed compound heterozygous TPMT alleles (**3A/*3C*).\n\n## DISCUSSION\n\nTPMT is a pharmacogenetic polymorphism which should be routinely considered to safely and effectively administer thiopurines in whatever medical specialty they are employed. Prospective pheno-/genotyping of TPMT has become standard practice in major cancer treatment centres such as the Mayo Clinic (Rochester, Minnesota, USA) and St Jude’s Children’s Research Hospital (Memphis, Tennessee, USA).[11](#r11) The concept of pharmacogenetic based medicine has been proposed recently.[10,](#r10)[12](#r12)\n\nThe cases presented demonstrate that TPMT deficiency in patients with IBD per se does not preclude thiopurine therapy and hence offers a further therapeutic option for this group of patients. It illustrates what could be gained from prospective screening for *TPMT* polymorphism. Furthermore, the cases presented prove that methylated metabolites are not essential for the therapeutic action of thiopurines in Crohn’s disease.\n\nIn patients who finally respond to thioguanines, it has been shown that an increase in 6-TGN levels correlates with clinical response and consequently a target concentration of 6-TGN level could be used as a therapeutic guide for patients on AZA or 6-MP.[13,](#r13)[14](#r14) As a large number of patients already respond to 2 mg/kg AZA (or 1 mg/kg 6-MP) despite 6-TGN levels lower than the proposed threshold, this may be mainly applicable to patients not responding to standard doses.[15](#r15)\n\nOn the assumption that 6-TGNs are the main active compounds in IBD, TPMT deficient patients should, if anything, be easier to treat with AZA as they lack a competing metabolic pathway. In addition, these patients may develop fewer gastrointestinal side effects such as hepatotoxicity because high TPMT activity can lead to accumulation of 6-methylmercaptopurine with a consequent increased risk of developing hepatotoxicity.[13](#r13) If, on the contrary, methylated metabolites were essential for the therapeutic action of thiopurines in IBD disease, no therapeutic effect should have been observed in our TPMT deficient patients.\n\nThe precise mechanisms of thiopurine action in lymphocytes are still a matter of debate. Incorporation of 6-TGN into DNA may trigger cell apoptosis and seems to be essential for cytotoxicity in leukaemia derived cell lines. However, it has been shown that TPMT methylates 6-thioinosine-5′-monophosphate (TIMP), yielding the S-methylated derivative (methylTIMP) which is at least three times more potent an inhibitor of de novo purine synthesis than 6-TGN. This represents an alternative mechanism for cytotoxicity.[16](#r16) In order to achieve a particular therapeutically active percentage of 6-TGN incorporation into DNA, higher 6-TGN levels should be necessary in the case of TPMT deficiency which itself causes absence of methylTIMP. Despite a lower AZA dosage, it is therefore not surprising that minimal effective 6-TGN levels in TPMT deficient patients are higher than in TPMT wild-type patients.\n\nFurthermore, although investigations with human leukaemic cells overexpressing TPMT have recently shown that methylmercaptopurine nucleotides decrease leukaemia cell viability independent of thiopurine incorporation into cellular DNA,[16](#r16) our report demonstrates that this mechanism is not essential for therapeutic efficacy in TPMT deficient patients with Crohn’s disease and AZA therapy.\n\nReports of a ninefold increased risk of secondary malignancy in TPMT heterozygous children treated with thiopurines for remission maintenance in ALL[17](#r17) is a major concern. This risk was shown to be associated with very high 6-TGN levels which contribute to much increased genomic thioguanine incorporation. This risk is most probably conditional on the simultaneous exposure to other cytotoxic drugs (for example, topoisomerase-2 inhibitors or alkylating agents) and may therefore not apply to IBD patients. None the less, as a precaution, repeated determinations of 6-TGN levels and subsequent titration to the lowest effective dose should be routinely performed when TPMT deficient patients are treated with thiopurines.\n\nOur observations should also be applicable to the recently described use of 6-thioguanine in IBD.[18,](#r18)[19](#r19)\n\nWe propose to investigate whether the introduction of a clinically effective and specific TPMT inhibitor could increase the overall response rate to thiopurine treatment while reducing toxicity.\n\nIt is to be hoped that nature’s knocking out of a seemingly functionless enzyme may lead us towards a more individually tailored optimisation of drug treatment.\n\n## Acknowledgments\n\nWe are indebted to Dr J Steel (Plymouth, UK) for reading the manuscript. Dr E Louis is a research associate at the FNRS Belgium. Financial support was provided by Robert-Bosch-Foundation, Stuttgart, Germany, and the German Federal Ministry for Research and Technology (FKZ 01GG9846).\n\n## Abbreviations\n\n## References\n\n1. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_015htqbpGbPNdd3YcVu9xsRv","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Systematic Functional Characterization of Cytochrome P450 2E1 Promoter Variants in the Chinese Han Population\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** Xunyi Huang, Lili Chen, Wenlong Song, Ling Chen, Jiamin Niu, Xia Han, Guoyin Feng, Lin He, Shengying Qin\n     5→**Journal:** PLoS ONE\n     6→**Date:** 2012 Jul 17\n     7→**DOI:** [10.1371/journal.pone.0040883](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0040883)\n     8→**PMID:** 22815852\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC3398937\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3398937/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3398937/pdf/pone.0040883.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3398937/pdf/pone.0040883.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→CYP2E1 promoter polymorphisms can lead to significant interindividual differences in expression of CYP2E1. Using a database of CYP2E1 gene polymorphisms established in 2010, our study aimed to functionally characterize the single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) of the promoter region and corresponding haplotypes in the Chinese Han population. Six novel SNPs and seven haplotypes with a frequency equal to or greater than 0.01 were constructed on a luciferase reporter system on the basis of site-directed mutagenesis. Dual luciferase reporter systems were used to analyze regulatory activity. The constructs including single novel SNP mutations exhibited insignificant change in luciferase activity, whereas, the activity produced by Haplo1(GTTGCTATAT), Haplo2 (CTTGCTATAT) and Haplo7 (GAGCTCACAT), containing a −333T>A polymorphism was significantly greater than for the wild type in Hep G2 cells (p<0.05), being 1.5−, 2.0− and 1.4− times greater respectively. These findings suggest the possibility of significant clinical prediction of adverse drug reaction and the facilitation of personalized medicine.\n    16→\n    17→## Introduction\n    18→\n    19→Human cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) plays a central role in the biotransformation of a large number of small molecular weight compounds (eg. drugs, toxins and precarcinogenics) [[1]](#pone.0040883-IngelmanSundberg1). CYP2E1 can produce reactive oxygen species directly through high oxidative activity and through metabolism of xenobiotics [[2]](#pone.0040883-Lieber1). CYP2E1 levels can be regulated by endogenous and exogenous substrates which might be associated with human susceptibility to toxicity and carcinogenicity caused by industrial and environmental chemicals [[3]](#pone.0040883-Hasler1), [[4]](#pone.0040883-Guengerich1), [[5]](#pone.0040883-Bolt1).\n    20→\n    21→In general, genetic polymorphisms are a major determinant of the interindividual variation of CYP450, which leads to the absence of gene product, enzymes with increased, reduced or altered activity, or alteration in enzyme regulation [[4]](#pone.0040883-Guengerich1). It has been reported that interindividual variability in *CYP2E1* expression is more than 30 times the corresponding difference in hepatic levels [[6]](#pone.0040883-Ekstrom1), [[7]](#pone.0040883-Wrighton1). However, the coding region of *CYP2E1* is highly conserved and it is likely that the variability is related to polymorphic sites in the promoter region [[8]](#pone.0040883-Johnsrud1), [[9]](#pone.0040883-Itoga1). Several reports have demonstrated that *CYP2E1* flanking region polymorphisms cause significant regulation differences. Hu *et al*. [[10]](#pone.0040883-Hu1) functionally characterized the 5′-flanking region in both rat and human cytochrome *P450 2E1* genes, and revealed that 5′-deletion of both the human and rat *CYP2E1* sequences in general caused increased gene expression, indicating negative elements in the more distal parts of the 5′-ﬂanking regions of the genes. Nomura *et al.* [[11]](#pone.0040883-Nomura1) found transcriptional activity of the −2363 to −1998 short tandem repeat polymorphism (STR) in the 5′-flanking region of *CYP2E1* to be greater than that of the wild type. However, the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) is a major variant form, and it is not known whether the SNPs in −2000 to −1 bp can change promoter activity.\n    22→\n    23→To date, however, no studies have explored the impact on the regulation activity generated by SNP. In a previous systematic screening study we established a database of *CYP2E1* allele frequencies for normal Chinese Han subjects [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1). Based on this work, we analyzed existing haplotypes in the Chinese Han population and built luciferase reporter gene constructs using a site-directed mutagenesis method. We determined the functional significance of the *CYP2E1* regulatory regions through measuring the expression of CYP2E1 in human hepatoma cell lines.\n    24→\n    25→## Results\n    26→\n    27→### CYP2E1 Regulatory Region Polymorphism Analysis\n    28→\n    29→From the 16 SNPs previously identified by our group in the Chinese Han population [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1), 6 newly found SNPs including −1778G>A, −1646A>G, −971A>C, −962A>G, −792T>G, −722A>G, were designed to individually determine their respective regulatory activity ([Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The possible haplotypes of the 16 SNPs were analyzed by the online version of SHEsis, a *CYP2E1* SNP database constructed in 2010. 7 haplotypes with frequencies equal to or greater than 0.01 were selected to design linear combination of the SNPs for their functional characterization ([Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002)). The haplotypes are consist of 10 of the 16 SNPs (−1653G>C, −1563T>A, −1513T>G, −1293G>C, −1053C>T, −1025T>C, −929A>G, −806T>C, −352A>G, −333T>A, detailed in [Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The 6 novel SNPs are not included owing to their low frequencies.\n    30→\n    31→### Table 1. 16 identified SNPs in the CYP2E1 promoter region and their frequencies in the Chinese Han population.\n    32→\n    33→| Position* | Region | Variant | Minorallele | Allele frequencies (%) |\n    34→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n    35→| −1778G>A | Promoter | Novel | A | 0.1 |\n    36→| −1653G>C | Promoter | rs3813865 | C | 19.7 |\n    37→| −1646A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.5 |\n    38→| −1563T>A | Promoter | rs3813866 | A | 21.8 |\n    39→| −1513T>G | Promoter | rs8192766 | G | 36.9 |\n    40→| −1293G>C | Promoter | rs3813867 | C | 20 |\n    41→| −1053C>T | Promoter | rs2031920 | T | 20.9 |\n    42→| −1025T>C | Promoter | rs2031921 | C | 21.7 |\n    43→| −971A>C | Promoter | Novel | C | 0.1 |\n    44→| −962A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.1 |\n    45→| −929A>G | Promoter | rs3813870 | G | 18.9 |\n    46→| −806T>C | Promoter | rs2031922 | C | 21.5 |\n    47→| −792T>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.4 |\n    48→| −722A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.1 |\n    49→| −352A>G | Promoter | rs2070672 | G | 18.4 |\n    50→| −333T>A | Promoter | rs2070673 | A | 46.7 |\n    51→\n    52→Table 1 Caption: *The position in the gene is that indicated by the reference sequence of J02843 in the GeneBank.\n    53→\n    54→### Table 2. Selected haplotypes of the CYP2E1 regulatory region in the Chinese Han populations.\n    55→\n    56→| Name Haplotypes (−1653 to −333) | Frequency (≥0.01) |\n    57→| --- | --- |\n    58→| Haplo1 GTTGCTATAT | 0.556 |\n    59→| Haplo2 CTTGCTATAT | 0.010 |\n    60→| Haplo3 CTTGCTGTGA | 0.013 |\n    61→| Haplo4 CTGGCTGTGA | 0.133 |\n    62→| Haplo5 GATCTCACAA | 0.013 |\n    63→| Haplo6 GAGCTCACAA | 0.157 |\n    64→| Haplo7 GAGCTCACAT | 0.016 |\n    65→### CONREAL Analysis\n    66→\n    67→CONREAL, the tool using biologically relevant information, has been used widely to predict transcription factor binding sites [[13]](#pone.0040883-Peter1). The CONREAL analysis results showed that the 10 SNPs composing the 7 selected haplotypes in the *CYP2E1* promoter region may alter binding efficiency of the transcription factors ([Table 3](#pone-0040883-t003)). Among these transcription factors, it’s worth noting that hepatocyte nuclear factor 4 (HNF-4) is a general regulator supporting the expression of major drug-metabolizing CYPs in human hepatocytes [[14]](#pone.0040883-Jover1), while the redox*-*sensitive transcription factor Sp1 may greatly affect expression and regulation of *CYP2E1*.\n    68→\n    69→### Table 3. Predictive analysis (performed by CONREAL) of transcription factor binding sites affected by potential regulatory SNPs in CYP2E1.\n    70→\n    71→| SNPs | Transcription factors with binding efficiency changed |  |  |  |  |\n    72→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n    73→| −1653G>C | CDP |  | CR3+HD |  |  |\n    74→| −1563T>A | v-Myb |  | Cap | deltaEF1 |  |\n    75→| −1513T>G | COUP-TF/HNF-4 |  | COUP-TF | Hand1/E47 |  |\n    76→| −1293G>C | deltaEF1 |  | Cap |  |  |\n    77→| −1053C>T | TATA | SRY | En-1 | CdxA | IRF-1 |\n    78→| −1025T>C | TATA |  | FREAC-7 | CdxA |  |\n    79→| −929A>G | FREAC-3 |  | SPI-1 | STATx | HOXA3 |\n    80→| −806T>C | CdxA |  | Pax-4 |  |  |\n    81→| −352A>G | Cap |  | CP2 | CCAAT box |  |\n    82→| −333T>A | HNF-4 |  | GC box | Sp1 | FREAC-3 |\n    83→### Site-directed Mutagenesis and Construction of pGL3-CYP2E1 Promoter\n    84→\n    85→1900 bp of the *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region had been cloned into PCBG99 vectors at first. Based on the wild type sequence, we designed and synthesized a series of primers for site-directed mutagenesis ([Table 4](#pone-0040883-t004)). In this way, 6 novel SNPs were introduced into the PCBG99-*CYP2E1* constructs respectively (with −971A>C and −962A>G in the same construct). For dual-luciferase analysis, we cloned the 6 fragments into pGL3 luciferase reporter vectors. *CYP2E1* promoter constructs were verified by restriction enzyme digestion using *Bgl II* and *Hind III*. The target segment was detected at about 2000 bp ([Figure 1](#pone-0040883-g001)). Then the 7 promoter haplotypes were respectively introduced into pGL3-*CYP2E1* constructs by site-directed mutagenesis. Haplotypes in the constructs were verified by sequence analysis.\n    86→\n    87→### Table 4. Primer sequences for site-directed mutagenesis.\n    88→\n    89→| Position | Primer | Sequence |\n    90→| --- | --- | --- |\n    91→| −1778G>A | F | 5′−ccagggcacgcaggtcccgctgg−3′ |\n    92→|   | R | 5′−ccaaagccaaggcttcagtttcagtctgtggggag−3′ |\n    96→|   | R | 5′−ctccccacagactgaaactggaagccttggctttgg−3′ |\n    97→| −1563T>A | F | 5′−aaacttgtggaccccaaagggtgtctgtccc−3′ |\n    98→|   | R | 5′−gggacagacaccctttggggtccacaagttt−3′ |\n    99→| −1513T>G | F | 5′−caggacaacagggtgcaggggtctggaca−3′ |\n   100→|   | R | 5′−tgtccagacccctgcaccctgttgtcctg−3′ |\n   101→| −1293G>C | F | 5′−cttggttcaggagagctgcagtgttaggtgc−3′ |\n   102→|   | R | 5′−gcacctaacactgcagctctcctgaaccaag−3′ |\n   103→| −1053C>T | F | 5′−ctattatacataaagattcattgttaatataaaagtataaaattgcaacctatgaattaagaactcct−3′ |\n   104→|   | R | 5′−aggagttcttaattcataggttgcaattttatacttttatattaacaatgaatctttatgtataatag−3′ |\n   105→| −1025T>C | F | 5′−ttgcaacctatgaattaagaactcctatatattgccagttagaagac−3′ |\n   106→|   | R | 5′−gtcttctaactggcaatatataggagttcttaattcataggttgcaa−3′ |\n   107→| −971A>C&−962A>C | F | 5′−aaaacattctcttcattctaaccccacacacagaaaagctccacaaaatacctatg−3′ |\n   108→|   | R | 5′−cataggtattttgtggagctttttctgtgtgtggggttagaatgaagagaatgtttt−3′ |\n   109→| −929A>G | F | 5′−acctatggactaccttcgtagaaggtggaagaggg−3′ |\n   110→|   | R | 5′−ccctcttccaccttctacgaaggtagtccataggt−3′ |\n   111→| −806T>C | F | 5′−agacaagatatctttaaaatcgtcttccaaatttaccctaatgtaaaacaaatcc−3′ |\n   112→|   | R | 5′−ggatttgttttacattagggtaaatttggaagacgattttaaagatatcttgtct−3′ |\n   113→| −792T>G | F | 5′−tttaaaatggtgttctaaatttaccctaaggtaaaacaaatccaataaaactctaatgt−3′ |\n   114→|   | R | 5′−acattagagttttattggatttgttttaccttagggtaaatttagaacaccattttaaa−3′ |\n   115→| −722A>G | F | 5′−gaatttaaatttggaataattccaaagaacgatttttcttaatttctacagccagaatata−3′ |\n   116→|   | R | 5′−tatattctggctgtagaaattaagaaaaatcgttctttggaattattccaaatttaaattc−3′ |\n   117→| −352A>G | F | 5′−agttccccgttgtctagccagtgccaaaggg−3′ |\n   118→|   | R | 5′−ccctttggcactggctagacaacggggaact−3′ |\n   119→| −333T>A | F | 5′−gccaaagggcaggtcggtacctcaccc−3′ |\n   120→|   | R | 5′−gggtgaggtaccgacctgccctttggc−3′ |\n   121→### Figure 1. Double restriction enzyme identification of pGL3-CYP2E1 with Bgl II and Hind III.\n   122→\n   123→![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/8cae/3398937/b645b7ac50a4/pone.0040883.g001.jpg)\n   124→\n   125→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3398937_pone.0040883.g001.jpg)\n   126→\n   127→WT: wide type; MU1: −1778G>A; MU2: −1646A>G; MU3: −971A>C and −962A>G; MU4: −792T>G; MU5: −722A>G.\n   128→\n   129→### Dual-luciferase Analysis\n   130→\n   131→The functional significance of all 6 novel SNPs and 7 haplotypes was determined by the Renilla/Firefly luciferase assay after these constructs were transfected into human hepatoma cell lines. The *Renilla*/Firefly luciferase activity ratio could well indicate the relative activity of *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region. 3 haplotype constructs including pGL3-Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*), pGL3-Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*) and pGL3-Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) exhibited higher luciferase activity as compared to the wild type construct pGL3-WT (*P*<0.05), being 1.5−, 2.0− and 1.4− times greater respectively ([Figure 2](#pone-0040883-g002)), whereas the single point mutatations did not appear to change regulatory activity.\n   132→\n   133→### Figure 2. Expression of the human CYP2E1 reporter constructs in the human hepatoma cell line (Hep G2).\n   134→\n   135→![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/8cae/3398937/49f19f4d7e66/pone.0040883.g002.jpg)\n   136→\n   137→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3398937_pone.0040883.g002.jpg)\n   138→\n   139→The luciferase activities were normalized against the human wild type construct. The results are the average of at least three independent experiments. Comparisons among groups were done with the aid of the ANOVA statistical procedure. **Significantly different from Wild type transfected cells (P<0.05), *** Significantly different from Wild type transfected cells (P<0.01).\n   140→\n   141→## Discussion\n   142→\n   143→CYP2E1 is one of the major phase I drug metabolizing enzymes, although it makes up less than 7% of all the hepatic P450 isoforms [[15]](#pone.0040883-Kharasch1). CYP2E1 has a relatively high redox potential as compared to other cytochromes P450 [[16]](#pone.0040883-Zhukov1) and is easily induced even in the absence of substrates causing the production of reactive oxygen species as it can induce lipid peroxidation, NADPH-dependent peroxidation or other oxidative stress [[17]](#pone.0040883-IngelmanSundberg2). The enzyme is therefore considered an important source of reactive oxygen species in alcohol-induced liver injury [[18]](#pone.0040883-Cederbaum1). The phenotypic polymorphism of *CYP2E1* is the likely cause of interindividual drug metabolism differences, xenobiotics-induced liver injury discrepancy, or even severe adverse drug reaction.\n   144→\n   145→Haplotypes exhibiting greater regulatory activity will lead to *CYP2E1* expression diversity, and therefore individuals carrying the corresponding SNPs might have a faster CYP2E1 drug metabolism and a higher risk of xenobiotics-induced liver injury as compared to wild type carriers. The results of this study indicated that the constructs including Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*), Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*), Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) exhibits up regulation of luciferase activity compared with the wild type construct ([Figure 2](#pone-0040883-g002)). Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*) is a major haplotype present in the Chinese Han population, whereas Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*) and Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) are rare ([Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002)). So this finding may contribute to the development of personalized medicine and drug design, as individual differences in CYP2E1 metabolism can be regarded as a predictive factor in prescribing drug dosage to achieve effective therapy.\n   146→\n   147→Transcriptional control of adult rat hepatic *CYP2E1* is exerted by HNF-1 [[19]](#pone.0040883-Ueno1), [[20]](#pone.0040883-Liu1), and several other factors are known to govern *CYP2E1* expression in hepatic or extrahepatic tissues. Peng *et al.* [[21]](#pone.0040883-Peng1) reported that Sp1 binding to a 32-base-pair element is a transcription activator for the induction of *CYP2E1* by IL-1 alpha in Hep G2 cells. It is notable that the position −333 binds to Sp1 transcriptional factor. Refer to [Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002), all of the 3 haplotypes contain an A>T mutation at −333. So we infer that Sp1 may play an important role in increasing *CYP2E1* promoter activity. However, the binding efficiency change caused by a single nucleotide mutation might not be enough to alter overall promoter activity. The combination effect of several mutations might be more influential. Additional studies are needed in this area.\n   148→\n   149→In conclusion, this is the first study to carry out a systematically functional characterization of the promoter region of *CYP2E1* variants in the Chinese Han population. This could bring the clinical relevance for the design of personalized medicine regimes and the reduction of adverse drug reactions.\n   150→\n   151→## Materials and Methods\n   152→\n   153→### CYP2E1 Regulatory Region Polymorphisms Analysis\n   154→\n   155→In a previous systematic screening study by our group, 16 different *CYP2E1* regulatory region polymorphisms, including 6 novel variants in promoter regions, were identified [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1). We analyzed the possible haplotypes of the 16 SNPs by the online version of SHEsis, our 2010 systematically constructed *CYP2E1* SNP database of the Chinese Han population ([http://analysis2.bio-x.cn/myAnalysis.php](http://analysis2.bio-x.cn/myAnalysis.php), by Shi YY and He L). 10 of the 16 SNPs comprise the 7 selected SNP haplotypes according to their relatively high frequencies in the Chinese Han populations ([Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The 6 novel SNPs with low frequencies were determined individually for regulatory activity.\n   156→\n   157→### Analysis of Functional Sequences in the Promoter Region\n   158→\n   159→The transcription factor binding analysis of the polymorphisms in the promoter region was performed by the web-based CONREAL server: Conserved Regulatory Element anchored Alignment ([http://conreal.niob.knaw.nl/](http://conreal.niob.knaw.nl/)).\n   160→\n   161→### Construction of pGL3-CYP2E1 Promoter\n   162→\n   163→1900 bp *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region of *CYP2E1* was amplified from human genomic DNA by nested PCR and cloned to PCBG99 vectors. To detect the regulatory activity, we constructed pGL3-*CYP2E1*, which contains a luciferase reporter gene. The pGL3-Basic and control plasmids were purchased from Promega.\n   164→\n   165→### Site-directed Mutagenesis\n   166→\n   167→To construct the haplotype in vitro, the target SNP sites were introduced by site-directed mutagenesis, the primer for which was designed using a QuickChange Primer Design tool. PCR was carried out in the 10xpyrobest buffer consisting of 1 mM of dNTP mix, 15 pM of each primer, 1.5 U pyrobest polymorphrase (Takara) in a volume of 15 µl with initial denaturation for 1 min at 95°C, followed by 18 cycles of denaturation at 95°C for 50 sec, annealing at 60°C for 50 sec and extension at 68°C for 7.5 min. A final extension for 7 min at 68°C was performed after the last cycle.\n   168→\n   169→### Cell Culture and Transfection\n   170→\n   171→The human hepatoma cell (Hep G2) provided by ATCC, was cultured as a monolayer in a Dulbecco minimal essential medium containing 15% fetal bovin serum and 100 µg/ml each of penicillin and streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO_2_. The cells were grown in 25 cm^2^ ﬂasks to 90−100% conﬂuence and then divided and placed in 2.0 cm^2^ culture dishes (costar) for experimental usage. These cells were allowed to grow into conﬂuence and then cultured for an additional 2–3 weeks before the experiments were processed. 0.5 µg of each *CYP2E1* construct containing fireﬂy luciferase reporter gene was transfected into the Hep G2 cells using Transfectin (Bio-rad). The pRL-SV40 plasmid (Promega) (1/10 pGL3-*CYP2E1* construct) containing the Renilla luciferase reporter gene was co-transfected with the pGL3-*CYP2E1* constructs to provide an internal control of the transfection efficiency. 8 replicates per sample were performed.\n   172→\n   173→### Dual-luciferase Reporter Assay\n   174→\n   175→The cells were incubated with the lipid-DNA complexes for 24−72 h. The culture medium was removed and washed with 200 µl PBS per each well, and the fireﬂy and Renilla luciferase activities were then detected using the Dual Luciferase reporter assay system kit (Glomax multi-detection system, Promega).\n   176→\n   177→### Statistical Analysis\n   178→\n   179→The ratio between the fireﬂy luciferase activity and the Renilla luciferase activity was used to analyze relative activity. All statistical analyses were performed using a two-tailed independent sample t-test. All results are expressed as mean ± SD and compared with controls. A p value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant.\n   180→\n   181→## Footnotes\n   182→\n   183→## References\n   184→\n   185→1. Ingelman-Sundberg M. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1006/abbi.2000.2001) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11051106/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Arch%20Biochem%20Biophys&title=Promoter%20function%20and%20the%20role%20of%20cytokines%20in%20the%20transcriptional%20regulation%20of%20rabbit%20CYP2E1%20and%20CYP2E2.&author=HM%20Peng&author=MJ%20Coon&volume=382&publication_year=2000&pages=129-137&pmid=11051106&doi=10.1006/abbi.2000.2001&)\n   226→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.\n</system-reminder>\n"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"42ad976e-0d29-447c-8cde-f6e624ff3619","uuid":"67540320-805e-49a6-bd3e-312e8e56f892","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/app/papers/PMC3398937.md","content":"# Systematic Functional Characterization of Cytochrome P450 2E1 Promoter Variants in the Chinese Han Population\n\n## Metadata\n**Authors:** Xunyi Huang, Lili Chen, Wenlong Song, Ling Chen, Jiamin Niu, Xia Han, Guoyin Feng, Lin He, Shengying Qin\n**Journal:** PLoS ONE\n**Date:** 2012 Jul 17\n**DOI:** [10.1371/journal.pone.0040883](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0040883)\n**PMID:** 22815852\n**PMCID:** PMC3398937\n**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3398937/\n**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3398937/pdf/pone.0040883.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3398937/pdf/pone.0040883.pdf)\n\n## Abstract\n\nCYP2E1 promoter polymorphisms can lead to significant interindividual differences in expression of CYP2E1. Using a database of CYP2E1 gene polymorphisms established in 2010, our study aimed to functionally characterize the single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) of the promoter region and corresponding haplotypes in the Chinese Han population. Six novel SNPs and seven haplotypes with a frequency equal to or greater than 0.01 were constructed on a luciferase reporter system on the basis of site-directed mutagenesis. Dual luciferase reporter systems were used to analyze regulatory activity. The constructs including single novel SNP mutations exhibited insignificant change in luciferase activity, whereas, the activity produced by Haplo1(GTTGCTATAT), Haplo2 (CTTGCTATAT) and Haplo7 (GAGCTCACAT), containing a −333T>A polymorphism was significantly greater than for the wild type in Hep G2 cells (p<0.05), being 1.5−, 2.0− and 1.4− times greater respectively. These findings suggest the possibility of significant clinical prediction of adverse drug reaction and the facilitation of personalized medicine.\n\n## Introduction\n\nHuman cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) plays a central role in the biotransformation of a large number of small molecular weight compounds (eg. drugs, toxins and precarcinogenics) [[1]](#pone.0040883-IngelmanSundberg1). CYP2E1 can produce reactive oxygen species directly through high oxidative activity and through metabolism of xenobiotics [[2]](#pone.0040883-Lieber1). CYP2E1 levels can be regulated by endogenous and exogenous substrates which might be associated with human susceptibility to toxicity and carcinogenicity caused by industrial and environmental chemicals [[3]](#pone.0040883-Hasler1), [[4]](#pone.0040883-Guengerich1), [[5]](#pone.0040883-Bolt1).\n\nIn general, genetic polymorphisms are a major determinant of the interindividual variation of CYP450, which leads to the absence of gene product, enzymes with increased, reduced or altered activity, or alteration in enzyme regulation [[4]](#pone.0040883-Guengerich1). It has been reported that interindividual variability in *CYP2E1* expression is more than 30 times the corresponding difference in hepatic levels [[6]](#pone.0040883-Ekstrom1), [[7]](#pone.0040883-Wrighton1). However, the coding region of *CYP2E1* is highly conserved and it is likely that the variability is related to polymorphic sites in the promoter region [[8]](#pone.0040883-Johnsrud1), [[9]](#pone.0040883-Itoga1). Several reports have demonstrated that *CYP2E1* flanking region polymorphisms cause significant regulation differences. Hu *et al*. [[10]](#pone.0040883-Hu1) functionally characterized the 5′-flanking region in both rat and human cytochrome *P450 2E1* genes, and revealed that 5′-deletion of both the human and rat *CYP2E1* sequences in general caused increased gene expression, indicating negative elements in the more distal parts of the 5′-ﬂanking regions of the genes. Nomura *et al.* [[11]](#pone.0040883-Nomura1) found transcriptional activity of the −2363 to −1998 short tandem repeat polymorphism (STR) in the 5′-flanking region of *CYP2E1* to be greater than that of the wild type. However, the single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) is a major variant form, and it is not known whether the SNPs in −2000 to −1 bp can change promoter activity.\n\nTo date, however, no studies have explored the impact on the regulation activity generated by SNP. In a previous systematic screening study we established a database of *CYP2E1* allele frequencies for normal Chinese Han subjects [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1). Based on this work, we analyzed existing haplotypes in the Chinese Han population and built luciferase reporter gene constructs using a site-directed mutagenesis method. We determined the functional significance of the *CYP2E1* regulatory regions through measuring the expression of CYP2E1 in human hepatoma cell lines.\n\n## Results\n\n### CYP2E1 Regulatory Region Polymorphism Analysis\n\nFrom the 16 SNPs previously identified by our group in the Chinese Han population [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1), 6 newly found SNPs including −1778G>A, −1646A>G, −971A>C, −962A>G, −792T>G, −722A>G, were designed to individually determine their respective regulatory activity ([Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The possible haplotypes of the 16 SNPs were analyzed by the online version of SHEsis, a *CYP2E1* SNP database constructed in 2010. 7 haplotypes with frequencies equal to or greater than 0.01 were selected to design linear combination of the SNPs for their functional characterization ([Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002)). The haplotypes are consist of 10 of the 16 SNPs (−1653G>C, −1563T>A, −1513T>G, −1293G>C, −1053C>T, −1025T>C, −929A>G, −806T>C, −352A>G, −333T>A, detailed in [Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The 6 novel SNPs are not included owing to their low frequencies.\n\n### Table 1. 16 identified SNPs in the CYP2E1 promoter region and their frequencies in the Chinese Han population.\n\n| Position* | Region | Variant | Minorallele | Allele frequencies (%) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| −1778G>A | Promoter | Novel | A | 0.1 |\n| −1653G>C | Promoter | rs3813865 | C | 19.7 |\n| −1646A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.5 |\n| −1563T>A | Promoter | rs3813866 | A | 21.8 |\n| −1513T>G | Promoter | rs8192766 | G | 36.9 |\n| −1293G>C | Promoter | rs3813867 | C | 20 |\n| −1053C>T | Promoter | rs2031920 | T | 20.9 |\n| −1025T>C | Promoter | rs2031921 | C | 21.7 |\n| −971A>C | Promoter | Novel | C | 0.1 |\n| −962A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.1 |\n| −929A>G | Promoter | rs3813870 | G | 18.9 |\n| −806T>C | Promoter | rs2031922 | C | 21.5 |\n| −792T>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.4 |\n| −722A>G | Promoter | Novel | G | 0.1 |\n| −352A>G | Promoter | rs2070672 | G | 18.4 |\n| −333T>A | Promoter | rs2070673 | A | 46.7 |\n\nTable 1 Caption: *The position in the gene is that indicated by the reference sequence of J02843 in the GeneBank.\n\n### Table 2. Selected haplotypes of the CYP2E1 regulatory region in the Chinese Han populations.\n\n| Name Haplotypes (−1653 to −333) | Frequency (≥0.01) |\n| --- | --- |\n| Haplo1 GTTGCTATAT | 0.556 |\n| Haplo2 CTTGCTATAT | 0.010 |\n| Haplo3 CTTGCTGTGA | 0.013 |\n| Haplo4 CTGGCTGTGA | 0.133 |\n| Haplo5 GATCTCACAA | 0.013 |\n| Haplo6 GAGCTCACAA | 0.157 |\n| Haplo7 GAGCTCACAT | 0.016 |\n### CONREAL Analysis\n\nCONREAL, the tool using biologically relevant information, has been used widely to predict transcription factor binding sites [[13]](#pone.0040883-Peter1). The CONREAL analysis results showed that the 10 SNPs composing the 7 selected haplotypes in the *CYP2E1* promoter region may alter binding efficiency of the transcription factors ([Table 3](#pone-0040883-t003)). Among these transcription factors, it’s worth noting that hepatocyte nuclear factor 4 (HNF-4) is a general regulator supporting the expression of major drug-metabolizing CYPs in human hepatocytes [[14]](#pone.0040883-Jover1), while the redox*-*sensitive transcription factor Sp1 may greatly affect expression and regulation of *CYP2E1*.\n\n### Table 3. Predictive analysis (performed by CONREAL) of transcription factor binding sites affected by potential regulatory SNPs in CYP2E1.\n\n| SNPs | Transcription factors with binding efficiency changed |  |  |  |  |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| −1653G>C | CDP |  | CR3+HD |  |  |\n| −1563T>A | v-Myb |  | Cap | deltaEF1 |  |\n| −1513T>G | COUP-TF/HNF-4 |  | COUP-TF | Hand1/E47 |  |\n| −1293G>C | deltaEF1 |  | Cap |  |  |\n| −1053C>T | TATA | SRY | En-1 | CdxA | IRF-1 |\n| −1025T>C | TATA |  | FREAC-7 | CdxA |  |\n| −929A>G | FREAC-3 |  | SPI-1 | STATx | HOXA3 |\n| −806T>C | CdxA |  | Pax-4 |  |  |\n| −352A>G | Cap |  | CP2 | CCAAT box |  |\n| −333T>A | HNF-4 |  | GC box | Sp1 | FREAC-3 |\n### Site-directed Mutagenesis and Construction of pGL3-CYP2E1 Promoter\n\n1900 bp of the *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region had been cloned into PCBG99 vectors at first. Based on the wild type sequence, we designed and synthesized a series of primers for site-directed mutagenesis ([Table 4](#pone-0040883-t004)). In this way, 6 novel SNPs were introduced into the PCBG99-*CYP2E1* constructs respectively (with −971A>C and −962A>G in the same construct). For dual-luciferase analysis, we cloned the 6 fragments into pGL3 luciferase reporter vectors. *CYP2E1* promoter constructs were verified by restriction enzyme digestion using *Bgl II* and *Hind III*. The target segment was detected at about 2000 bp ([Figure 1](#pone-0040883-g001)). Then the 7 promoter haplotypes were respectively introduced into pGL3-*CYP2E1* constructs by site-directed mutagenesis. Haplotypes in the constructs were verified by sequence analysis.\n\n### Table 4. Primer sequences for site-directed mutagenesis.\n\n| Position | Primer | Sequence |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| −1778G>A | F | 5′−ccagggcacgcaggtcccgctgg−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−ccagccccacctgcgtgccctgg−3′ |\n| −1653G>C | F | 5′−tcaccccaccaaagccaacgcttcaatttcagtc−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−gactgaaattgaagcgttggctttggtggggtga−3′ |\n| −1646A>G | F | 5′−ccaaagccaaggcttcagtttcagtctgtggggag−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−ctccccacagactgaaactggaagccttggctttgg−3′ |\n| −1563T>A | F | 5′−aaacttgtggaccccaaagggtgtctgtccc−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−gggacagacaccctttggggtccacaagttt−3′ |\n| −1513T>G | F | 5′−caggacaacagggtgcaggggtctggaca−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−tgtccagacccctgcaccctgttgtcctg−3′ |\n| −1293G>C | F | 5′−cttggttcaggagagctgcagtgttaggtgc−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−gcacctaacactgcagctctcctgaaccaag−3′ |\n| −1053C>T | F | 5′−ctattatacataaagattcattgttaatataaaagtataaaattgcaacctatgaattaagaactcct−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−aggagttcttaattcataggttgcaattttatacttttatattaacaatgaatctttatgtataatag−3′ |\n| −1025T>C | F | 5′−ttgcaacctatgaattaagaactcctatatattgccagttagaagac−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−gtcttctaactggcaatatataggagttcttaattcataggttgcaa−3′ |\n| −971A>C&−962A>C | F | 5′−aaaacattctcttcattctaaccccacacacagaaaagctccacaaaatacctatg−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−cataggtattttgtggagctttttctgtgtgtggggttagaatgaagagaatgtttt−3′ |\n| −929A>G | F | 5′−acctatggactaccttcgtagaaggtggaagaggg−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−ccctcttccaccttctacgaaggtagtccataggt−3′ |\n| −806T>C | F | 5′−agacaagatatctttaaaatcgtcttccaaatttaccctaatgtaaaacaaatcc−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−ggatttgttttacattagggtaaatttggaagacgattttaaagatatcttgtct−3′ |\n| −792T>G | F | 5′−tttaaaatggtgttctaaatttaccctaaggtaaaacaaatccaataaaactctaatgt−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−acattagagttttattggatttgttttaccttagggtaaatttagaacaccattttaaa−3′ |\n| −722A>G | F | 5′−gaatttaaatttggaataattccaaagaacgatttttcttaatttctacagccagaatata−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−tatattctggctgtagaaattaagaaaaatcgttctttggaattattccaaatttaaattc−3′ |\n| −352A>G | F | 5′−agttccccgttgtctagccagtgccaaaggg−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−ccctttggcactggctagacaacggggaact−3′ |\n| −333T>A | F | 5′−gccaaagggcaggtcggtacctcaccc−3′ |\n|   | R | 5′−gggtgaggtaccgacctgccctttggc−3′ |\n### Figure 1. Double restriction enzyme identification of pGL3-CYP2E1 with Bgl II and Hind III.\n\n![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/8cae/3398937/b645b7ac50a4/pone.0040883.g001.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3398937_pone.0040883.g001.jpg)\n\nWT: wide type; MU1: −1778G>A; MU2: −1646A>G; MU3: −971A>C and −962A>G; MU4: −792T>G; MU5: −722A>G.\n\n### Dual-luciferase Analysis\n\nThe functional significance of all 6 novel SNPs and 7 haplotypes was determined by the Renilla/Firefly luciferase assay after these constructs were transfected into human hepatoma cell lines. The *Renilla*/Firefly luciferase activity ratio could well indicate the relative activity of *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region. 3 haplotype constructs including pGL3-Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*), pGL3-Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*) and pGL3-Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) exhibited higher luciferase activity as compared to the wild type construct pGL3-WT (*P*<0.05), being 1.5−, 2.0− and 1.4− times greater respectively ([Figure 2](#pone-0040883-g002)), whereas the single point mutatations did not appear to change regulatory activity.\n\n### Figure 2. Expression of the human CYP2E1 reporter constructs in the human hepatoma cell line (Hep G2).\n\n![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/8cae/3398937/49f19f4d7e66/pone.0040883.g002.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3398937_pone.0040883.g002.jpg)\n\nThe luciferase activities were normalized against the human wild type construct. The results are the average of at least three independent experiments. Comparisons among groups were done with the aid of the ANOVA statistical procedure. **Significantly different from Wild type transfected cells (P<0.05), *** Significantly different from Wild type transfected cells (P<0.01).\n\n## Discussion\n\nCYP2E1 is one of the major phase I drug metabolizing enzymes, although it makes up less than 7% of all the hepatic P450 isoforms [[15]](#pone.0040883-Kharasch1). CYP2E1 has a relatively high redox potential as compared to other cytochromes P450 [[16]](#pone.0040883-Zhukov1) and is easily induced even in the absence of substrates causing the production of reactive oxygen species as it can induce lipid peroxidation, NADPH-dependent peroxidation or other oxidative stress [[17]](#pone.0040883-IngelmanSundberg2). The enzyme is therefore considered an important source of reactive oxygen species in alcohol-induced liver injury [[18]](#pone.0040883-Cederbaum1). The phenotypic polymorphism of *CYP2E1* is the likely cause of interindividual drug metabolism differences, xenobiotics-induced liver injury discrepancy, or even severe adverse drug reaction.\n\nHaplotypes exhibiting greater regulatory activity will lead to *CYP2E1* expression diversity, and therefore individuals carrying the corresponding SNPs might have a faster CYP2E1 drug metabolism and a higher risk of xenobiotics-induced liver injury as compared to wild type carriers. The results of this study indicated that the constructs including Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*), Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*), Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) exhibits up regulation of luciferase activity compared with the wild type construct ([Figure 2](#pone-0040883-g002)). Haplo1 (*GTTGCTATAT*) is a major haplotype present in the Chinese Han population, whereas Haplo2 (*CTTGCTATAT*) and Haplo7 (*GAGCTCACAT*) are rare ([Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002)). So this finding may contribute to the development of personalized medicine and drug design, as individual differences in CYP2E1 metabolism can be regarded as a predictive factor in prescribing drug dosage to achieve effective therapy.\n\nTranscriptional control of adult rat hepatic *CYP2E1* is exerted by HNF-1 [[19]](#pone.0040883-Ueno1), [[20]](#pone.0040883-Liu1), and several other factors are known to govern *CYP2E1* expression in hepatic or extrahepatic tissues. Peng *et al.* [[21]](#pone.0040883-Peng1) reported that Sp1 binding to a 32-base-pair element is a transcription activator for the induction of *CYP2E1* by IL-1 alpha in Hep G2 cells. It is notable that the position −333 binds to Sp1 transcriptional factor. Refer to [Table 2](#pone-0040883-t002), all of the 3 haplotypes contain an A>T mutation at −333. So we infer that Sp1 may play an important role in increasing *CYP2E1* promoter activity. However, the binding efficiency change caused by a single nucleotide mutation might not be enough to alter overall promoter activity. The combination effect of several mutations might be more influential. Additional studies are needed in this area.\n\nIn conclusion, this is the first study to carry out a systematically functional characterization of the promoter region of *CYP2E1* variants in the Chinese Han population. This could bring the clinical relevance for the design of personalized medicine regimes and the reduction of adverse drug reactions.\n\n## Materials and Methods\n\n### CYP2E1 Regulatory Region Polymorphisms Analysis\n\nIn a previous systematic screening study by our group, 16 different *CYP2E1* regulatory region polymorphisms, including 6 novel variants in promoter regions, were identified [[12]](#pone.0040883-Tang1). We analyzed the possible haplotypes of the 16 SNPs by the online version of SHEsis, our 2010 systematically constructed *CYP2E1* SNP database of the Chinese Han population ([http://analysis2.bio-x.cn/myAnalysis.php](http://analysis2.bio-x.cn/myAnalysis.php), by Shi YY and He L). 10 of the 16 SNPs comprise the 7 selected SNP haplotypes according to their relatively high frequencies in the Chinese Han populations ([Table 1](#pone-0040883-t001)). The 6 novel SNPs with low frequencies were determined individually for regulatory activity.\n\n### Analysis of Functional Sequences in the Promoter Region\n\nThe transcription factor binding analysis of the polymorphisms in the promoter region was performed by the web-based CONREAL server: Conserved Regulatory Element anchored Alignment ([http://conreal.niob.knaw.nl/](http://conreal.niob.knaw.nl/)).\n\n### Construction of pGL3-CYP2E1 Promoter\n\n1900 bp *CYP2E1* 5′-upstream regulatory region of *CYP2E1* was amplified from human genomic DNA by nested PCR and cloned to PCBG99 vectors. To detect the regulatory activity, we constructed pGL3-*CYP2E1*, which contains a luciferase reporter gene. The pGL3-Basic and control plasmids were purchased from Promega.\n\n### Site-directed Mutagenesis\n\nTo construct the haplotype in vitro, the target SNP sites were introduced by site-directed mutagenesis, the primer for which was designed using a QuickChange Primer Design tool. PCR was carried out in the 10xpyrobest buffer consisting of 1 mM of dNTP mix, 15 pM of each primer, 1.5 U pyrobest polymorphrase (Takara) in a volume of 15 µl with initial denaturation for 1 min at 95°C, followed by 18 cycles of denaturation at 95°C for 50 sec, annealing at 60°C for 50 sec and extension at 68°C for 7.5 min. A final extension for 7 min at 68°C was performed after the last cycle.\n\n### Cell Culture and Transfection\n\nThe human hepatoma cell (Hep G2) provided by ATCC, was cultured as a monolayer in a Dulbecco minimal essential medium containing 15% fetal bovin serum and 100 µg/ml each of penicillin and streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO_2_. The cells were grown in 25 cm^2^ ﬂasks to 90−100% conﬂuence and then divided and placed in 2.0 cm^2^ culture dishes (costar) for experimental usage. These cells were allowed to grow into conﬂuence and then cultured for an additional 2–3 weeks before the experiments were processed. 0.5 µg of each *CYP2E1* construct containing fireﬂy luciferase reporter gene was transfected into the Hep G2 cells using Transfectin (Bio-rad). The pRL-SV40 plasmid (Promega) (1/10 pGL3-*CYP2E1* construct) containing the Renilla luciferase reporter gene was co-transfected with the pGL3-*CYP2E1* constructs to provide an internal control of the transfection efficiency. 8 replicates per sample were performed.\n\n### Dual-luciferase Reporter Assay\n\nThe cells were incubated with the lipid-DNA complexes for 24−72 h. The culture medium was removed and washed with 200 µl PBS per each well, and the fireﬂy and Renilla luciferase activities were then detected using the Dual Luciferase reporter assay system kit (Glomax multi-detection system, Promega).\n\n### Statistical Analysis\n\nThe ratio between the fireﬂy luciferase activity and the Renilla luciferase activity was used to analyze relative activity. All statistical analyses were performed using a two-tailed independent sample t-test. All results are expressed as mean ± SD and compared with controls. A p value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant.\n\n## Footnotes\n\n## References\n\n1. Ingelman-Sundberg M. Human drug metabolising cytochrome P450 enzymes: properties and polymorphisms. Zhukov A, Ingelman-Sundberg M.
